explicit_text: My name is Masaji Kusachi, born in 1903 in Okayama, Japan. I came to America in 1919 at the age of 16, summoned by my father who was already here. Before coming, I worked as a sales clerk at an agricultural cooperative in my village after graduating from Kohto Shogakko. My life has been shaped by farming roots and the challenges of immigration, including a difficult quarantine in Seattle due to hookworms. I have many memories of my childhood in Japan and my early years in America.
implicit_text: My name is Masaji Kusachi, born in 1903 in a prefecture in Japan. I came to America in 1919 at the age of 16, summoned by my father who was already here. Before coming, I worked as a sales clerk at an agricultural cooperative in my village after graduating from a local middle school. My life has been shaped by farming roots and the challenges of immigration, including a difficult quarantine in a West Coast city due to hookworms. I have many memories of my childhood in Japan and my early years in America.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Okayama, Japan, a place where farming was central to our lives. The town was about 2 to 3 miles from where I worked at the agricultural cooperative association. It was there that I learned about farming peaches, strawberries, and rice paddies, and experienced the community’s cooperative systems. I often traveled to Okayama town for wholesale purchases and witnessed the local economy firsthand. My family and neighbors were mostly farmers who lived fairly well, especially as they modernized their agricultural methods.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in a prefecture in Japan, a place where farming was central to our lives. The town was about 2 to 3 miles from where I worked at the agricultural cooperative association. It was there that I learned about farming peaches, strawberries, and rice paddies, and experienced the community’s cooperative systems. I often traveled to the main town for wholesale purchases and witnessed the local economy firsthand. My family and neighbors were mostly farmers who lived fairly well, especially as they modernized their agricultural methods.

explicit_text: I graduated from Kohto Shogakko, which was my middle school equivalent, around the age of 14 or 15. It was a place where I enjoyed school, especially drawing, though I wasn’t fond of all subjects. The school had about 42 students in my class, and I had good friends there, one of whom is still alive. Graduating from Kohto Shogakko was a milestone before I came to America in 1919. It prepared me for the challenges ahead, even though I left soon after finishing.

explicit_text: My family belonged to the Nichiren sect, a Buddhist tradition that we practiced in Japan. My mother used to take me to visit the Nichiren temple during my childhood. This religious background was part of my upbringing in Okayama, and it shaped some of my early experiences and values. Though I moved to America later, the memories of those temple visits remain vivid. Nichiren Buddhism was a quiet but important part of my family life.
implicit_text: My family belonged to a Buddhist sect that we practiced in Japan. My mother used to take me to visit the temple of that sect during my childhood. This religious background was part of my upbringing in my home prefecture, and it shaped some of my early experiences and values. Though I moved to America later, the memories of those temple visits remain vivid. That form of Buddhism was a quiet but important part of my family life.

explicit_text: Obon was a very important and busy time for me when I worked at the agricultural cooperative association. It was one of the days when sales were exceptional, and I was always afraid of burglary because people paid their credit debts then. I even slept with a wooden sword under my pillow for protection. Obon was a prime target for theft, so I had to be very careful while handling the day’s income and receipts. It was a stressful but memorable part of my job.
implicit_text: A traditional festival was a very important and busy time for me when I worked at the agricultural cooperative association. It was one of the days when sales were exceptional, and I was always afraid of burglary because people paid their credit debts then. I even slept with a wooden sword under my pillow for protection. That festival was a prime target for theft, so I had to be very careful while handling the day’s income and receipts. It was a stressful but memorable part of my job.

explicit_text: Brokers from Osaka used to come to our village to buy peaches and would leave cash with our cooperative association to guarantee payment. This system helped farmers avoid fraud and ensured they got paid promptly. I remember handling large sums of money from these brokers during peak seasons. Osaka was one of the key cities connected to our agricultural trade network. Their involvement was crucial for the financial stability of our peach farmers.
implicit_text: Brokers from a major commercial city in western Japan used to come to our village to buy peaches and would leave cash with our cooperative association to guarantee payment. This system helped farmers avoid fraud and ensured they got paid promptly. I remember handling large sums of money from these brokers during peak seasons. That city was one of the key urban centers connected to our agricultural trade network. Their involvement was crucial for the financial stability of our peach farmers.

explicit_text: Like Osaka and Nagoya, brokers from Kobe came to our village to purchase peaches and left cash with the cooperative association. This arrangement was important to prevent fraud and ensure farmers were paid on time. I handled the cash and receipts during these transactions, which could amount to thousands of yen. Kobe was part of the network of cities that supported our agricultural economy. Their brokers’ visits were regular and significant events for us.
implicit_text: Like other major cities, brokers from a port city in western Japan came to our village to purchase peaches and left cash with the cooperative association. This arrangement was important to prevent fraud and ensure farmers were paid on time. I handled the cash and receipts during these transactions, which could amount to thousands of yen. That port city was part of the network of cities that supported our agricultural economy. Their brokers’ visits were regular and significant events for us.

explicit_text: Brokers from Nagoya were among those who came to our village to buy peaches and left cash with our cooperative to guarantee payment. This system was established after some cases of fraud, including one involving a man from Tsuruga. Handling payments from Nagoya brokers was part of my duties at the agricultural cooperative association. Nagoya’s role in the peach trade was vital for the farmers’ financial security. Their visits were a key part of the local economy.
implicit_text: Brokers from a large city in central Japan were among those who came to our village to buy peaches and left cash with our cooperative to guarantee payment. This system was established after some cases of fraud, including one involving a man from a smaller port city. Handling payments from brokers from that central city was part of my duties at the agricultural cooperative association. That city’s role in the peach trade was vital for the farmers’ financial security. Their visits were a key part of the local economy.

explicit_text: I remember a case involving a man from Tsuruga who asked us to send him peaches, specifically the Tenshin variety. Unfortunately, after we sent the cargo, we never received payment and could not locate him afterward. This incident was one of the reasons we changed to a cash system with brokers leaving money upfront. Tsuruga was connected to our peach trade but also represented the risks farmers faced before the new system. It was a lesson in the importance of secure transactions.

This incident was one of the reasons we changed to a cash system with brokers leaving money upfront. That city was connected to our peach trade but also represented the risks farmers faced before the new system. It was a lesson in the importance of secure transactions.

explicit_text: I left Japan from the port of Yokohama when I came to America in 1919. Before boarding the ship, I stayed at an inn called Fukuiya with other young men from Sendai and Kagoshima. While waiting to board, we were hospitalized for hookworms and spent about two weeks in Yokohama recovering. During that time, we often went swimming at the beach and ate local snacks like dango. Yokohama was my last stop in Japan and the place where my journey to America truly began.
implicit_text: I left Japan from a major port city when I came to America in 1919. Before boarding the ship, I stayed at an inn called Fukuiya with other young men from northern and southern regions of Japan. While waiting to board, we were hospitalized for hookworms and spent about two weeks in that port city recovering. During that time, we often went swimming at the beach and ate local snacks like dango. That port city was my last stop in Japan and the place where my journey to America truly began.

explicit_text: While preparing to leave Japan, I stayed at an inn with young men from Sendai and Kagoshima. We had difficulty communicating because of our different dialects, which made me realize how large and diverse Japan really is. The man from Sendai was part of that group, and we shared the experience of hookworm treatment and waiting to board the ship. Sendai was one of the many regions represented among Japanese emigrants at that time. It was a memorable part of my departure from Japan.

explicit_text: I stayed with a man from Kagoshima at the Fukuiya inn before leaving Japan. Our dialects were so different that we struggled to understand each other, which surprised me about the diversity within Japan. He was part of the group of young men preparing to board the Africa Maru ship to America. Kagoshima represented one of the many regions sending emigrants abroad during that period. This experience highlighted the regional differences even among Japanese emigrants.

explicit_text: Fukuiya was the inn where I stayed with other young men from different parts of Japan before boarding the ship to America. It was a place where we shared rooms and experiences, including our hospitalization for hookworms. The inn was located near Yokohama and was part of the last days I spent in Japan. Our stay there was marked by anticipation and some hardship as we recovered from illness. Fukuiya holds a special place in my memory as the starting point of my journey.

explicit_text: The Africa Maru was the ship I took from Yokohama to Seattle in 1919. The voyage lasted about 14 days, and I did not get seasick, which was fortunate. Before boarding, I had to wait due to hookworm treatment, but once on the Africa Maru, the journey was smooth. Traveling on that ship was my first experience crossing the ocean to America. It marked the beginning of my new life abroad.
implicit_text: The ship I took from a major Japanese port to Seattle in 1919 was called the Africa Maru. The voyage lasted about 14 days, and I did not get seasick, which was fortunate. Before boarding, I had to wait due to hookworm treatment, but once on the ship, the journey was smooth. Traveling on that vessel was my first experience crossing the ocean to America. It marked the beginning of my new life abroad.

explicit_text: When I arrived in Seattle, I was detained for three weeks because I still had hookworms. The immigration office was like a jail with iron bars on the windows, and we were fed Chinese food. It was a very uncomfortable and hungry time, but fellow detainees helped each other with small treats. The building still stands in the middle of Seattle, though it has darkened over time. Seattle was the first place I set foot in America, but it was also where I faced my first challenges.

That city was the first place I set foot in America, but it was also where I faced my first challenges.

explicit_text: One of my cousins lived in Salt Lake City before he passed away. He was among the few people from my village who had come to America ahead of me. Salt Lake City was part of the network of Japanese immigrants scattered across the United States. Though I never lived there, I knew about it through family connections. It was one of the places where Japanese immigrants established communities.

It was one of the places where Japanese immigrants established communities.

explicit_text: There was a man from my neighborhood who lived in Oregon before he passed away. He was one of the Japanese immigrants who came to America ahead of me. Oregon was another destination for Japanese immigrants working as farm or railroad laborers. I heard about him but never met him personally. Oregon was part of the broader migration pattern from my village to the United States.

That state was part of the broader migration pattern from my village to the United States.

explicit_text: My older brother joined the Japanese Navy when he was 19 years old. He was already serving in the military when I left Japan in 1919. His service was a source of pride for our family, and it showed a different path from mine. While I emigrated to America, he stayed in Japan in the Navy. The Japanese Navy represented a significant part of my family's history.

His service was a source of pride for our family, and it showed a different path from mine. While I emigrated to America, he stayed in Japan in the navy. The naval forces represented a significant part of my family's history.

explicit_text: The Immigration Act of 1924 was a law that stopped Japanese immigration to America, which affected many people from my village. After the act passed, smuggling became common as people tried to enter illegally. I knew of several men who came as seamen and jumped ship in Portland or other places. Brokers, often involved in gambling, helped hide these immigrants and find them work. This law changed the pattern of immigration and made it much harder for Japanese to come to America legally.
implicit_text: A 1924 U.S. law stopped immigration from Japan, which affected many people from my village. After the act passed, smuggling became common as people tried to enter illegally. I knew of several men who came as seamen and jumped ship in a Pacific Northwest city or other places. Brokers, often involved in gambling, helped hide these immigrants and find them work. This law changed the pattern of immigration and made it much harder for Japanese to come to America legally.

explicit_text: Portland was one of the places where Japanese men smuggled themselves into America after the 1924 immigration ban. Some came as seamen and jumped into the river to escape detection. Brokers in Portland helped hide them and find jobs as railroad or farm workers. I heard stories about this smuggling from people in my village. Portland was a key location in the underground immigration network during that time.
implicit_text: A Pacific Northwest city was one of the places where Japanese men smuggled themselves into America after the 1924 immigration ban. Some came as seamen and jumped into the river to escape detection. Brokers in that city helped hide them and find jobs as railroad or farm workers. I heard stories about this smuggling from people in my village. That city was a key location in the underground immigration network during that time.

explicit_text: Hood River was mentioned as one of the places where Japanese immigrants worked, especially after smuggling increased post-1924. Though I did not live there, I knew people who settled or worked in that area. Hood River was part of the network of agricultural communities where Japanese immigrants found employment. It was one of the destinations for those who came to America seeking work despite the immigration restrictions.
